name: Coffee
description: Beans, brews, and baristas — from Kaldi's goats in Ethiopia to the global chains of today.

questions.q: The two primary species of commercial coffee bean are Arabica and which other?

source.original_a: C. arabica and C. robusta beans
source.notes: Arabica is ~60% of global supply.

questions.q: Which country is the world's largest coffee producer by volume?

source.notes: Brazil has led global production for 150+ years.

questions.q: A shot of espresso diluted with hot water is commonly called what?

source.notes: Named by American soldiers diluting European espresso.

questions.q: In which country is the global coffee chain Starbucks headquartered?

source.notes: Seattle, Washington — founded 1971.

questions.q: The beans that become coffee start life as fruit. What are those fruits called?

source.original_a: Fruits of the coffee tree, grown in tropical regions like Southeast Asia and South America
source.notes: The 'bean' is actually the seed of the cherry.

questions.q: Which traditional espresso-based drink layers equal espresso, steamed milk, and milk foam?

source.notes: Named after the Capuchin friars' robes.

questions.q: Caffeine is a stimulant that primarily blocks which neurotransmitter receptor?

source.notes: Blocking adenosine receptors keeps you feeling alert.

questions.q: Which Ethiopian goatherd is legendarily said to have discovered coffee's stimulating effect?

source.notes: The legend is 9th-century.

questions.q: In what century did coffee first spread into the Islamic world?

source.notes: First documented in Yemen's Sufi monasteries.

questions.q: Where did the first coffeehouse in Europe open in 1647?

source.notes: London's first coffeehouse followed in 1652.

questions.q: Which iconic American drip-coffee innovation was introduced by Nestlé in 1938?

source.notes: Developed for the Brazilian government to preserve surplus beans.

questions.q: Which unbelievably expensive coffee involves beans passing through the digestive tract of a civet?

source.notes: Produced mostly in Indonesia and the Philippines.

questions.q: Robusta beans contain how much more caffeine than Arabica, approximately?

source.notes: audit-corrected 2026-05-09: Robusta ~2.2-2.7% vs Arabica ~1.2-1.5% caffeine — roughly 2x, not 3x. Distractors retuned.

questions.q: What is the name for coffee beans that have been harvested but not yet roasted?

source.notes: Greenish color; shelf-stable for roasting.

questions.q: Which method traps grinds below a plunger while hot water steeps?

source.notes: French press — aka 'cafetière.'

questions.q: What chemical reaction browns coffee beans during roasting and gives them their flavor?

source.notes: Caramelization also contributes, but the Maillard reaction is dominant.

questions.q: Which stovetop coffee maker, invented in Italy in 1933, uses steam pressure to push water upward through a filter basket?

source.notes: Invented by Alfonso Bialetti in Italy. Bialetti is the brand; Moka pot is the type.

questions.q: Turkish-style coffee is traditionally prepared in what type of small pot?

source.notes: Long-handled copper or brass pot; Ottoman origin.

questions.q: Which Yemeni port city gave its name to a coffee drink blended with chocolate?

source.notes: Mocha is named for the port of Mokha (al-Mukha) on the Red Sea.

questions.q: Coffee was famously banned or restricted in Europe by which 17th-century figure who later (legend says) 'baptized' it as a Christian drink?

source.notes: Around 1600; legend that he blessed it after tasting.

questions.q: Which coffee roast level produces the darkest, shiniest beans — often called 'Italian' or 'French'?

source.notes: Dark roasts have less caffeine and acidity than light.

questions.q: Decaffeinated coffee was invented in 1903 by a German merchant named Ludwig Roselius. He marketed it under what brand name?

source.notes: 'Kaffee Hag' in Germany; 'Sanka' in France and the U.S.
